g.data-narration: Welcome to our exploration of the Central Limit Theorem and Confidence Intervals, the foundation of statistical inference.
In data science and computer science, we often need to infer population characteristics from limited samples. This module shows us how.
g.text: In real-world CS applications, we rarely have access to entire populations.
The CLT guarantees we can make reliable inferences about populations from samples.

g.data-narration: We will explore three main areas: the Central Limit Theorem, Confidence Intervals using Z and t distributions, and a practical worked example.
The Central Limit Theorem states that the sampling distribution of means becomes approximately normal as sample size increases, regardless of population shape.
g.text: "If you take sufficiently large random samples from ANY population with mean μ and
finite variance, the distribution of sample means (x̄) will be approximately normal."

g.data-narration: For CLT to apply, we need sample size n ≥ 30, random sampling, and independent observations. The mean of sample means equals population mean μ.
The Standard Error (SE = σ/√n) measures how sample means vary around the population mean. Larger samples reduce sampling error.
g.text: The standard deviation of the sampling distribution is called the Standard Error.

g.data-narration: A confidence interval provides a range of values likely to contain the population mean, with a specified level of confidence like 95%.
g.text: The CI provides an estimate with a margin of error, quantifying uncertainty in our inference.

g.data-narration: When population sigma is known, we use the Z-distribution. For 95% confidence, Z = 1.96. The formula is CI = x̄ ± Z × SE.
g.text: For 95% confidence: α = 0.05, α/2 = 0.025, so Zα/2 = 1.96

g.data-narration: In reality, sigma is usually unknown. We use sample standard deviation s and t-distribution with n-1 degrees of freedom for more accurate intervals.
g.text: When σ is Unknown: Use t-Distribution
t-distribution has heavier tails than normal, accounting for uncertainty from estimating σ with s.

g.data-narration: t-critical values depend on degrees of freedom (n-1). As sample size increases, t approaches Z. For n≥30, they're quite similar.
g.text: df = n - 1. As n increases, tα/2 approaches Zα/2 (1.96 for 95%)

g.data-narration: Let's work through an example: analyzing database query response times. We have a sample of 40 queries with x̄ = 152ms and s = 28ms.
g.text: A data engineer analyzes response time of a new database query.
Goal: Construct a 95% confidence interval for the true mean response time.

g.data-narration: Step by step: We check CLT applies (n≥40), find t-critical for df=39, calculate SE = 4.42, and margin of error = 8.95.
The 95% confidence interval is (143.05, 160.95) milliseconds. We're confident the true mean lies within this range.
g.text: 95% confident true mean is in this range

g.data-narration: Let's summarize the key formulas: Standard Error, Z-based CI, and t-based CI for confidence intervals.
The CLT enables statistical inference on any population. Use Z when sigma is known, t when unknown. Larger samples yield narrower, more precise intervals.
